title: Le futur proche
description: The near future: aller + infinitive, for talking about plans and what's about to happen.

cards.notes: The engine of the futur proche. Conjugate **aller** in the present, then add an infinitive: *Je **vais** manger* — I'm going to eat.
*Je vais **voyager** en France* — I'm going to travel to France.
Used for places: *visiter Paris*. For people you use *rendre visite à*. *Nous allons **visiter** le musée.*
*Tu vas **regarder** un film ?* — Are you going to watch a movie?
Starts with a vowel. *Elle va **étudier** ce soir* — She's going to study tonight.
*Je vais **rencontrer** mes amis* — I'm going to meet my friends.
*Ils vont **manger** une pizza* — They're going to eat a pizza.
Time marker that pairs naturally with the futur proche: *Je vais voyager **demain**.*
*Nous allons étudier **ce soir*** — We're going to study tonight.
French borrows *week-end* from English (with a hyphen). *On va visiter Paris **ce week-end**.*
*prochaine* = next. *Je vais rencontrer Marie **la semaine prochaine**.*
Note the circumflex on the **ô**. *On va se rencontrer **bientôt*** — We're going to meet soon.

steps.title: Talking about plans
steps.body:
# Le futur proche

To say what you're **going to do** — your plans, what's about to happen — French uses the *futur proche* ("near future"). It works exactly like the English *going to*:

> **aller** (conjugated in the present) + **INFINITIVE**

- *Je **vais manger**.* — I'm going to eat.
- *Tu **vas voyager** ?* — Are you going to travel?
- *Elle **va étudier**.* — She's going to study.

The second verb stays in its **infinitive** form — you never change it. All the work happens on *aller*.

You already met *aller* (to go) earlier in the course. Here it stops meaning "go" literally and becomes the signal for the near future.

steps.title: Conjugating aller
steps.body:
## The present tense of aller

| Subject | aller | + infinitive |
|---|---|---|
| je | **vais** | je vais voyager |
| tu | **vas** | tu vas visiter |
| il / elle | **va** | elle va étudier |
| nous | **allons** | nous allons manger |
| vous | **allez** | vous allez regarder |
| ils / elles | **vont** | ils vont rencontrer |

*aller* is irregular — learn these six forms by heart. Once you know them, the futur proche is easy: just attach any infinitive.

Watch the spelling traps: it's *je **vais***, not *je vai*; *il **va***, not *il vais*; *ils **vont***, not *ils vais*.

exercise.prompt: Match each French infinitive with its English meaning.
Match each form of *aller* with the subject it goes with.

steps.title: Build: We're going to visit Paris

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *We're going to visit Paris this weekend.*
exercise.hint: *Nous* + form of *aller* + infinitive + place + time marker.

steps.title: I am going to travel tomorrow

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I am going to travel tomorrow.* (Use the futur proche.)

steps.title: Saying you're NOT going to
steps.body:
## Negation: wrap aller, not the infinitive

To say you're *not* going to do something, put **ne ... pas** around *aller* (the conjugated verb). The infinitive stays put at the end.

> subject + **ne** + aller + **pas** + infinitive

- *Je **ne vais pas** travailler demain.* — I'm not going to work tomorrow.
- *Elle **ne va pas** étudier ce soir.* — She's not going to study tonight.
- *Ils **ne vont pas** manger ici.* — They're not going to eat here.

Remember the elision: *ne* becomes **n'** before a vowel — *Je **n'**ai pas* (but here *aller* starts with a consonant, so it stays *ne vais pas*).

## Time markers for the future

These pair naturally with the futur proche:

- **demain** — tomorrow
- **ce soir** — tonight
- **ce week-end** — this weekend
- **la semaine prochaine** — next week
- **bientôt** — soon

steps.title: Which means 'she is going to study'?

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly means *She is going to study*?
exercise.hint: *elle* takes *va*, and the second verb must stay in its infinitive form (*étudier*).

steps.title: They are going to eat tonight

exercise.prompt: Translate: *They are going to eat tonight.* (Use masculine *they*.)

steps.title: Build: I'm not going to work tomorrow

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *I'm not going to work tomorrow.*
exercise.hint: *ne ... pas* wraps *vais* (the conjugated verb); the infinitive stays after *pas*.

steps.title: We are going to meet soon

exercise.prompt: Translate: *We are going to meet soon.* (A natural way uses *se rencontrer*.)
exercise.hint: *Nous allons nous rencontrer* or the more casual *On va se rencontrer* + *bientôt*.

exercise.prompt: Match each French time expression with its English meaning.

steps.title: Build: You're going to watch a film

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *You're going to watch a film tonight.* (informal *tu*)
exercise.hint: *tu* takes *vas*; the infinitive *regarder* follows, then object and time.
